frameworks.name: natural input statistics
frameworks.one_line: Weber scaling emerges from correlations among naturally-scaled sensory measurements.

frameworks.name: noisy neural coding
frameworks.one_line: Standard account attributing Weber's law to neuronal noise is challenged.

frameworks.name: Bayesian inference
frameworks.one_line: Discrimination framed as inference over scaled sensory inputs.

core_question: Can Weber's law arise from natural input statistics without invoking neural noise?
key_assumptions: Sensory measurements are scaled by global variables (contrast, loudness).
Global scaling induces positive correlations among measurements.
Discrimination reflects inference over these correlated inputs.
manipulations: Theoretical variation of global scaling parameters across natural inputs.
key_findings: Global scaling parameters induce positive correlations among sensory measurements.
These correlations produce Weber scaling of discrimination thresholds.
Predicts approximately log-normal tuning curves for speed and numerosity.
interpretations: Weber's law is an unavoidable consequence of natural sensory statistics, not neural noise.
Noisy neurons are not required to explain Weber's scaling.
